text: The surgeon dissects at the hiatus, working anteriorly on the right cruce of the diaphragm to separate the esophagus. A large vessel crossing through the pars flaccida limits visualization.

The surgeon clamps a vessel suspected to be a replaced left hepatic artery and injects three milligrams of ICG. The endocyanin green fluorescence angiography reveals ICG filling the lungs and then the peripheral tissues.

The surgeon observes that the left liver lobe is not lighting up green, indicating a lack of perfusion. The CAUT-VIII and right liver are bright green, showing good vascularization. The surgeon unclamps the vessel and continues to free tissue in the pars flaccida to gain length on the vessel. After releasing the vessel, the left liver lobe lights up green, confirming the need to preserve the vessel.
